sentences.text: Right there my last story would take a year.
[LAUGHS]
I mentioned I was in a place, you know, that took the little kids, the infants, you know, and knock down the wall and throw them like--
Already?
OK.
So my cousin got angry and he look at me and he says, you want some more of which you got one time before?
You remember?
I think I already said it.
So I said, no way you know?
I'm not the same person you knew years back.
I'm strong now, and I'm not a Muselmann.
I say, if you are a man, I said, just come out of the office and we talk out.
He said to the guy hold my jacket, I give the guy another lesson.
I said-- before I was in Dachau, I used to box for six years.
I used to box people much older than me.
I started boxing when I was ten years through the war started, you know?
I was very good with my hands.
So the guy come over there.
[INAUDIBLE] again I threw him a left and a right.
You know what happened.
I threw him a couple more.
He's bleeding from his nose, from his mouth.
Before he knows it, he was down on the floor.
I start kicking him just the way the German used to beat me.
So then at the-- then he said, stand up.
I got enough.
I got enough.
I say, [INAUDIBLE].
I give you 48 hours.
I don't want see- I don't want you staying in Salzburg.
I want you to leave town.
If you don't I say, I'm going to report you.
Was an American rabbi with couple of lines, I don't know what they call them, that, you know, a rabbi by an American soldier.
Anybody knew something, did something bad in the concentration camp, we can report it to him, and he was going to do something about it.
I knew two, three guys where I can report him.
I say, listen I gave you a chance, I say.
You better leave town.
Otherwise they're going to deport you to the American-- I don't know how they call those people, you know?
They come with the soldiers to do service in the war.
So in two days he picked everything up and he went to Italy.
From there I don't know what happened to him, if he's alive.
He was going to go to Palestine.
If he's dead or alive I don't know what happened to him.
So was the end of this cousin.
So then I stayed in Salzburg.
I was trying to go to Palestine.
Two people I had transport I had and they got captured, and they put them in Cyprus.
Is an island they used to pick on the people who wanted to go to Palestine.
Who captured them?
The British.
They was not so good to the Jews, you know?
Some of them-- they was always as bad as Nazi Jew haters.
Not all of them, but some.
So they used to put them in Cyprus in a Greek island.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I could not go there.
So then I started doing a lot of bad things over there.
Pick on purpose fight with the Germans, you know?
I was a-- me, I was having blood in my eyes, you know?
And so when I see my eyes just to do something to hurt the Germans.
Anything under the book.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
After a few months later I was there-- so a guy from Romania, who was living in Palestine, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he come there and the camp we were living now we used to call it New Palestine.
He come, was checking around to find out some young guys was not afraid to die, or afraid to do something.
He wants to enjoy to come in there going.
He had a long talk with me.
He says to me-- in the beginning I don't want to go.
To tell you the truth, I was very involved with a lot of girls over there.
So, you know, I was not married yet, you know?
A young guy, you know, having fun with the young Frauleins over there.
And then he says to me, listen, he says, look what it did to your parents.
Look what it did to your family, blah, blah, blah.
We have to build a place for our own, to have a land for us, you know?
We have to make Palestine to be for ours.
Not belong to the Arabs and to the British.
We have to do something for us.
He start talking me in and talking to me until finally he convinced me.
I say, all right.
I going to do that.
Then I ask, what do you want to do?
I'll let you know what to do.
He used to get orders from Palestine.
He was working with the same group as Menachem Begin.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I-- he was the only contact I had.
I don't know nobody else.
And we're not supposed to tell to nobody.
Everything to keep secret from everybody.
What do you do.
Even my closest friend not supposed to know.
Everything's supposed to be alone.
Because we swear, you know, on the Torah.
We don't going to say nothing.
So then he get me my job.
Because I spoke Polish and Russian.
He said, you're going to go to Poland.
There's quite a few Jews that want to come to Palestine.
You're going to help them to cross the borders.
You're very sharp in that, you know.
And you help them to cross the borders to bring them to the American side.
And from there we can take them over.
Because where the Russians on watch it's very hard to get out.
Matter of fact, Russia used to-- was in Hungary at the time, part of Vienna, and the whole Poland was occupied from the Russian, you know?
And people want to get out, they cannot get out.
So I went.
I went with another guy.
They gave us each one a gun.
We went there.
They give us money too, in case we have to buy off those guards crossing the border.
So we went to where-- we had a place where we're going to be meet them, the people.
We already give the place, the address and everything.
Somebody else was working from the other side.
You see?
Used to work in groups.
One part here, this was my job, and somebody else should take over, because one person cannot take all the way down from the-- from Poland take all the way down to Palestine.
Each one used to have his own place.
So when we got-- we got a group for there, about 25 people, all ages.
Little kids, older people, middle class, women, all kinds.
Where was this?
Was in Poland.
So we took them there.
In the beginning we don't know, so finally, you know, we cross them, across the border, with no problems the first time.
Well, at night.
On foot.
Of course, on foot.
Once you cross the American border, you go to the American side, there's no problems.
Over there on the Russian side was bad.
So once we cross the border we took them to the American side.
So my part was as far as sometimes to leave them in Germany where the American watch, or sometimes take them to Austria.
So we cross over there no problems.
A few times we run into trouble.
Couple of times, matter of fact, we run into Jew Russian soldiers, you know?
We spoke Yiddish I say, guy, guy, guy.
He says, go fast, because if they catch him, you know, they can get in trouble.
If they catches us we can get in big trouble too.
And a couple times we got problems.
Sometimes we used to give them a case of vodka, you know, to share it out.
Most of the soldiers look around then and let it go.
So we did it maybe about 20 times maybe.
So sometimes when I'm in trouble we had to do what we have to do to get it over.
To take them over the border.
So I have a picture from then when I transferred, if you want to see it.
I have when we arrive in the American side.
We stopped in a forest everybody was freezing to death, and light a big fire there to warm it up.
And I was watching around.
I had my gun in the hand.
You know, looking around if somebody's going to come over or something.
If I do whatever I do to save the life from those people, because it was my responsibility.
But in the beginning didn't trust me.
They thought I was too young to have such a responsible job.
So after awhile there they put me in a different type of job.
They wanted me to organize to bring arms to Palestine.
So I started around to see what we can do, you know?
That was around 1947, something that.
And where were you living?
I was living in Salzburg.
But when people knew me, what kind of person I was, everybody wanting my wife not to marry me.
Not to marrying, because I was a very violent person.
And I was very crazy.
Everybody told her that.
Stay away from that guy.
He's violent and he's crazy.
Because, you know, sometimes people hear what I did.
But at that time I didn't give a damn about my life.
I saw death millions of time.
Got nothing to fear, you know?
At least I know I'm doing something to get even with some people that hurt my family.
So we start taking arms to them, because I speak Italian pretty good.
I got to know an Italian fisherman.
We pay him good.
So what we did is we put the arms-- it had a hole on the bottom, and put the guns on the bottom, and the fish on the top.
And we used to-- I never went inside in Israel.
We used to dump in some place over there and some were waiting for us.
You know, you give them the signal?
We dump because the British-- the British was watching so close, you know, that they were very mean.
They catch somebody they put you away and in prison and all kinds of things.
They were not so nice to the Jews, the British soldiers.
I mean, the patrol.
I did this for quite a while.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then--
Almost before Israel become a state.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I got captured one time.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they was going to convict me.
And they put us-- me and another guy, they put us in a prison in Cyprus.
That's where they used to keep all the time over there.
Because I knew the language pretty good.
The Greeks language.
It was-- I went to school in Greece.
So I got to know.
I start talking with the guards over there-- blah, blah, blah, blah, you know?
And somebody helped me with money.
I escaped from prison.
I don't know how long I was going to get.
I escaped from prison.
I went to Italy.
From Italy I went to Salzburg, and I said to my wife, we have to leave here.
I live in disguise for a little while.
Then I went-- my wife-- and I come to the United States under my wife's name.
Because I was-- people were looking for me everywhere.
And if you look at the passport, the citizenship, you're going to see my wife first and I'm second.
Usually the husband in the front then the husband.
So we escaped and come to the United States, in 19-- 1949.
Well, before we go on with that, can you tell me how you met your wife?
And what the DP camp was like in Salzburg.
I met my wife in the New Palestine camp.
I met her, and, you know, and we fell in love.
We got married.
And now we have three kids.
I want to have a dozen, but she doesn't agree with me.
I cannot do all by myself.
[LAUGHS] You know?
We live in the United States now.
I got this number in Birkenau.
And my-- Daniel, the one who got hanged, got one number ahead of me.
And the older brother David was another number ahead.
But David I saw only one time in the camp, and I never saw him again.
But Daniel and I was always together till they hang him.
When I got the number?
I got the number the same day we arrive in Birkenau.
And then from there they give us a cold shower.
So we don't know what it was.
One side went in the shower for the people that was still gas.
And one time, which instead of water used to come gas out.
And gas them out.
And we heard screaming and hollering but we don't know what the heck it was.
From the other building was for the people who was going to put to work.
So I was one of the lucky ones where they put me to work.
You know, used to call them the death camp.
When I was-- when I was in Birkenau-- yes, I do remember one thing.
When I was in Birkenau I saw taking little kids and knocking on the wall, and like the thing knocking them brains out.
And one more thing I forgot one time.
First thing when I come in there, in Birkenau, I still I was strong a few weeks later.
All of a sudden they pick me up from there, and they took me to a barrack, me and another 20 guys.
I don't know how many it was.
Younger guys, between 16 and 18, 19.
Still I was-- I was no food but it took me a lot to come back to a skin and bones, you know, while still I had some meat in me.
They took men in a barrack and they gave us good food there and everything, you know?
And the doctors used to check us every time.
Then one day we were working there we were sitting laying on the-- in the sun, no work, no nothing I said this is heaven.
Compared to what I was doing before.
So I find out there was the experiment cabin.
They was going to make experiment on us.
What kind, I don't know.
We were going to be guinea pigs.
So of a sudden come in big officer.
He saw everybody in there, he start screaming and hollering, get up!
[GERMAN]!
So we get up and they took us to a train to unload some different stuff from a train, took us over there.
So I was lucky.
They never took me back to the experiment place.
They send me back to the barrack.
I was very close to be experimented.
What they was going to do to me, I had no idea, because they used to do experiment there.
Dr.
Mengele and other doctors used to make experiment in this same place.
So and I got away with it.
But I liked it in the beginning, because there was food and everything to eat and no hard labor, and no beating and everything.
The doctor used to come everyday.
Used to check us, see if everything is OK.
I say, what is that?
This is not Birkenau.
This is heaven.
So later on we find out what the place was for.
Yes, I just remember that.
How'd you find that out?
I hear it from other people where they got the experiment there.
You know, some people they told me about horrible things they did to them.
They cut some part of the man thing, see if we can have sex.
Some have twins try to do different things, you know?
I find out, you know, after the camp, you find out things, you know, what you don't know when you was in the camp.
You know what I mean?
After that, you find out a lot of things.
Now, like you just find out what's going on.
At the time in the camp I don't know those things, you know?
After talking to people, oh, you was there?
Yes, I got a surgery there.
Matter of fact, there's a woman here who went through the camp here too.
She can have no children.
I know she's married, but she can't have no kids.
They did some kind of experiment on her.
She was in the same block.
I was lucky.
I think it was block 29.
I'm not sure, but I think it was 29.
Yeah.
It was closed with the wires all around, and there was like a red thing on the top, like a blue cross-- a red cross.
You know, and it looks like a hospital from the outside.
We got good food, good everything, and I was there a short time.
Maybe about a week to two weeks there.
Then they took us out and they put us back to work again.
So in a way it's good and a way it's--
Pardon me?
Medical treatment?
Was such a thing in medical treatment over there?
I got lice running all over me.
They used to eat me alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, there was no hospitals too.
Everybody was sick, it was goodbye Charlie.
The only one that are on their feet can stand and work, they still want.
If I wasn't going sticking to Warsaw, I never was going to make it.
And I was very young and strong when I got in, and I'd become like a living skeleton, you know?
My legs was as thick as my arm here.
It's a much-- we lost the weight.
Everybody become skeletons.
There's no food, you work hard, you get breathing, you know?
When you have some meat they hit you, you don't feel it so much when they hit you.
You got bones, that hurts.
It goes straight to the bone.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's the way it goes.
All right.
Yes?
Yes.
I was a policeman in the camp.
Close to the camp we had some Ukrainian and Russian soldiers where they threw their arms and they went to the German side.
And they went to the German side, and they were afraid to go back to Russia because they're going to execute them over there, you know?
It was about two-- about a mile away from our camp.
And they used to come inside in our camp at night and attack older people-- steal their watches, their rings, whatever they can get, and beat them up.
So we organized about 10 guys of us, the strong guys.
Everybody's going to watch around the camp, you know, to see around.
And sometimes we used to see trespassing, we used to give them such a beating when he never could put his foot there to protect the people inside the camp.
Was about 500 people in the camp.
So I did job to us-- I was up besides-- because not always I used to go-- I used to go for the trips, you know?
Sometimes I used to stay two, three weeks, you know, stay home to see if everything is OK.
To watch around.
How many guards is watching the borders and blah, blah, blah, you know?
We have to be prepared before you make a such a decision, because was too many lives involved in it, you know?
They can get hurt.
They get in big trouble, including me.
Who knows?
Probably they send me to Siberia or something.
You know, we were crossing the borders, and the Russians don't play games.
They were very strict in those things.
So and that's why I was a policeman there.
Watching over the people in the camp.
Me and a few other more people.
So.
Well, actually, that was my home.
But I was there from 19-- mid 1945 till 1949-- '48, but from there I had to skip and went to Germany because I was afraid they was going to catch me when I escaped from the prison.
So I lived in Germany for awhile in disguise.
Then I come with my wife's name into the United States.
And when we come here I had a child, was six months old.
So-- you want to hear a little thing of what happened in the ship?
Yeah.
OK.
I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I went on the ship-- the ship's name was Was a soldiers ship.
We used to-- about 100 people, they used to live in a thing, beds up and down, you know?
The women with children and the older senior citizens, they used to put them in a cabin, three or four people bunk beds.
My oldest daughter, she was a colic baby.
She used to cry a lot.
I don't know what was wrong with her.
She used to cry 24 hours a day.
So we went on the ship that-- inside the ship.
After one or two days the women right there, they're all senior citizens, was screaming at my wife, says kill this little baby.
We cannot sleep.
Day and night, she cries, blah, blah, blah, you know?
My oldest daughter.
So my wife, she says to me, come out and says, Albert, she says, you know, I need a little sleep.
Why don't you take the baby once in a while?
You know, to watch the baby?
I say, OK.
I do that.
So I'll go to your room.
And over there was a sign on the door. 'Not allowed' was written in English.
None men allowed to go to where the females was.
Because was a big ship, you know?
Were about 2,000 people were inside the ship.
And all coming to America.
They are from different nationalities.
And the headmen, they want to transfer-- [INAUDIBLE] they call him, you know?
He was a British guy, and I know him from the camp before.
In the same time [INAUDIBLE], before that, they hang a couple of British in Israel.
They hang a couple of British soldiers.
And he was so much against the Jews, that guy, the headman.
But he was just to bring the people from Europe and he used to go back again with the same ship.
That's why he was [INAUDIBLE].
And he spoke German pretty good.
So I saw this sign over there, but I don't understand English, why it says not allowed to go in for the women.
So I went anyway.
So two MPs saw me going in.
And says to me, no, no.
No.
I say, what, no, no, no?
I go-- in German, I tell them, I say [GERMAN].
They spoke to me English, I spoke to them in German.
They say, no, no no.
So, OK, they threw me out.
They don't let me go inside, because was small corridors to go in, you know?
So I went around the corner and I watching, and I watching him to go around, because I have to go take the baby, give my wife a break.
One of them saw me, that I went and sneak in.
And the guy after he saw me, and he attack me, you know?
So he push me.
So I gave him one push and the guy flew down like a fly.
One of the MPs.
He had MP written down.
The other soldier's-- whistle.
Before you know I had four or five other guys coming around me.
They all attack me, you know?
We wrestle for awhile.
Finally, they handcuff me.
They took me to the captain of the ship.
Took me to the captain on the ship.
And he started talking to me.
I say, no speak English.
Then they call up the big guy, the British guy over there, to come over.
To be an interpreter.
And he was speaking German to me.
And I say, to him, the whole thing I did, I say, my wife is sick.
The baby's crying.
I come to give my wife a relief, to take her up on the deck, you know, where she can sleep a little bit.
We are three days on this ship, I say.
She cannot sleep.
Says to me, don't you know, he says, it says forbidden to come in the ladies rooms?
I say, I don't speak English.
I don't read English.
I don't know what you want me to do.
I say, it's against-- but I know.
Then he said the captain, for the captain from the ship, he says to me, you dare attack one of the guards.
I say, well, he attacked me.
I tried to defend myself.
He says to me, what we going to do is, he says to me, we're going to put you-- it will take 13 days on the ship to go there.
The next following 10 days we're going to put you in jail.
They got a jail inside the ship.
Or, you're going to do errands around here.
You're going to work around the ship here.
I say, what are we?
In the Nazi land?
I say, I be coming to the United States, a free country.
So the guy push me, I push him back.
I say, I don't kill nobody.
What's wrong with you guys?
So he said, here's another problem.
He says, you're talking back to the captain.
You've got a big mouth.
I say, what the hell?
I do nothing.
Says to me, take it or leave it, he says.
You're going to be 10 days in prison, or you're going to work 10 days around the ship.
I figure, you know, better than nothing, I work 10 days around the ship.
But in the meantime I had an upset stomach, you know?
I don't know.
The food did not agree with me and everything.
And the ship was up and down, you know?
So the following morning they call me on the loud speaker, Albert Rosa report to the office.
Ah, screw them.
So the next day again, Albert Rosa.
Nothing.
When they arrive in New Orleans, the guy was looking for me around.
I saw him, was hiding.
The big British guy.
You got to think-- I mean, because I got away with that.
So he was looking for me, I was hiding.
So when I went come over there to-- and he was holding my papers to come down from the ship to land in the United States legal.
When I went-- everybody was a few tables over there, and they ask you-- ask for your name and they gave you the paper.
So and so gave you the paper.
That way the guy on the front that looks, you know, some-- how you call those people?
Somebody legal was sneak in the United States, you know?
They check-- they wanted the paper.
Then the one on the door, they check you again, you know?
Say your wife or daughter, whatever you have.
When I went over there he says-- the captain-- the transport [INAUDIBLE] got the papers.
I went to him and I say, how come you're holding my papers?
Don't you remember?
We told you have to work on the ship or else you're not coming to the United States.
We're going to take you back with us to Europe.
I say, you're kidding.
He say, your wife and your daughter can stay here, but you have to come back.
You're going to go back.
I say, you must be kidding.
I say, what's the matter with you?
You talk like I killed somebody.
Well, this is another way.
I say, what do you want me to do?
He says, you're going to take the blankets from all the beds and you're going to throw in a place-- the [NON-ENGLISH] they call it, for [NON-ENGLISH], for lice.
In a big machine, like a washing machine.
Hot.
You got to throw the blankets over there, you know?
And that's what you're going to do.
Was another few-- two, three other guys, you know, in my position.
So I went and took a bunch of blankets, I put on my shoulder and I showed to him.
That's what you want me to do?
Yes.
That's what you want to do.
Keep going, he says to me.
But then after he saw me going, he gave the paper to the guy on the table over there.
So I went and I took the blankets, I threw wherever he told me.
I went around the ship, because I know the ship like my hand.
I was there 13 days.
It was a big ship I know every door, everything.
I went over there and I say the transfer [INAUDIBLE] told me, it's OK now.
He gave me the papers.
He gave me the papers to me, Albert Rosa, he says.
Oh, yeah.
He gave me the papers back.
I'm supposed to work at it all day.
My wife's supposed to wait for me.
I took the papers and I went down.
And he was upstairs on the third floor.
He was looking down.
And I hollered at him, hey schmuck.
[INAUDIBLE] I holler after him in German going, hey, schmuck!
The guys turn around.
He says, who?
You, schmuck!
He look down and he says, how you got there?
I say, [GERMAN].
He cannot come.
He can do nothing.
I already am in the United States.
He got so mad.
He started punching.
He can do nothing.
So we come to new Orleans.
